headline: HOGS RUN 11%, HORMUZ REIGNITES, CORN SPLITS
subheadline: Weekend U.S.-Iran escalation reopened the Strait of Hormuz premium, lean hogs had their biggest session in weeks, and nearby corn fell hard while December held.
lead: The weekend wasn't quiet. U.S. strikes on Iran, Iran striking five Gulf nations, and claims the Strait of Hormuz is closed again hit the tape before Monday's open, and the market sorted out winners and losers fast. Lean hogs ran 10.8% to $94.78, their biggest single session in recent memory, on a move nobody had priced Friday. Nearby corn fell hard to $4.38 while December held at $4.61, a 23-cent split that is the market's live pollination-weather calculator, with USDA Crop Progress coming at 3:00 PM CT today to tell us whether that premium has earned its place.
the_takeaway: Hogs moved; Hormuz reopened; wait for 3 PM crop progress before touching corn.
teaser: Iran closed the Strait again overnight. Hogs ran 11%. Corn split in two. Today's crop progress report decides which way the grain complex leaves the range.

one_number.unit: nearby vs. Dec corn carry gap at today's close
one_number.context: Nearby corn fell to $4.38 while December held at $4.61, a 23-cent split that has widened sharply into pollination week. That gap is the market embedding a weather discount into old crop while new crop holds its nerve. If crop progress this afternoon shows good/excellent above 65%, that carry narrows fast. If it prints below 60%, December could push toward $4.75.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called corn up toward $4.61 December, with nearby holding above $4.38 as the pollination-weather premium built into new crop.
yesterdays_call.note: Corn closed at $4.38 nearby and $4.61 December -- the level was reached on the December contract but the nearby fell hard to $4.38, not up. Direction on December was right, but the call as written implied both contracts firming; nearby went the wrong way. Missed. Own it.

sections.title: Corn Splits at Pollination
sections.body: Nearby corn fell hard to **$4.38**, down 14.5 cents on the session, while December held at **$4.61**, up 8.5 cents. That 23-cent gap between contracts is the market pricing old-crop delivery pressure against new-crop pollination risk, and both sides moved in the same session. The catalyst is the USDA Crop Progress report at 3:00 PM CT today: the 2026 planting season showed corn at 42% as of the week ending May 24, with the Belt running ahead of the five-year average since. Pollination stress in the next two weeks is the only story that justifies holding December above $4.60. **If today's condition rating prints below 60% good/excellent, December has room toward $4.75. Above 65%, nearby pressure wins and the spread compresses.** The market is positioned for the number, not ahead of it.
sections.catalyst: USDA Crop Progress report at 3 PM CT; corn pollination window now open.
sections.bottom_line: Hold new-crop corn decisions until 3 PM. The report resolves the split.
sections.farmer_action: Unpriced new-crop corn: hold through today's crop progress. Condition rating below 60% good/excellent opens the door toward $4.75 December; above 65%, scale sales on the first 10-15% increment above $4.65.

sections.title: Hogs Had Their Biggest Day
sections.body: Lean hogs ran to **$94.78**, up 10.8%, their biggest single-session move in months, and nothing in Friday's close telegraphed it. This is an overnight surprise with a specific driver: Iran's weekend strikes on Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, and Jordan, the U.S.-Iran re-escalation, and the Strait of Hormuz closing again lifted every risk-on commodity that has a short-term demand or export story. Pork exports move through different lanes than LNG, but funds rotating into commodities with export leverage found hogs. The CME Group also announced two new beef trim futures contracts Friday, a structural signal that the cattle supply chain is adding risk-management tools, and that's creating positive sentiment across the protein complex. **$94.78 is the highest hog print in weeks.** The question is whether this holds Tuesday or gives back half the move when the Hormuz headlines stop being new news.
sections.catalyst: U.S.-Iran weekend escalation, Strait of Hormuz closure claims, risk-on fund rotation into protein exports.
sections.bottom_line: Real move, real catalyst, but 11% in a day demands skepticism about follow-through.

sections.title: Wheat Firms, Still Below $6.45
sections.body: Chicago wheat moved to **$6.32**, up 12.25 cents (2%), continuing to hold the gains built off last Thursday's WASDE. The WASDE showed a smaller U.S. crop estimate and lower old-crop stocks, and the market hasn't given that back. **$6.45 is the next real resistance** and wheat hasn't closed above it. Friday's session also held, and producers with old-crop in storage who scaled sales above $6.30 per the July 10 window got a clean fill. The move today has Hormuz fingerprints on it too: European natural gas prices opened 3% higher Monday on Hormuz escalation, and anything tied to energy cost and global grain logistics tightened with it. Soybeans firmed to **$11.96** nearby and **$11.91** November, with soybean meal adding $7.70 to **$323.10** and soybean oil pushing to **$70.86**. The crush complex is confirming bean demand, not just chart action.
sections.catalyst: WASDE small U.S. crop estimate holding; Hormuz re-escalation tightening global grain logistics.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat and beans firming together. $6.45 is the wheat line that hasn't broken yet.
sections.farmer_action: Old-crop wheat still in storage: $6.32 is a real price. If carry costs are running, take the bid. Scale additional sales on a close above $6.40.

sections.title: Cattle Mixed; Hormuz Adds Noise
sections.body: Live cattle firmed to **$235.20**, up 1.6%, holding the line the complex has defended since the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout, which began May 19 and has removed roughly 2% of weekly U.S. slaughter capacity with no resolution announced. Feeders slipped to **$354.60**, down 0.5%, which means the feeder/live spread is still wide and finishing margins are still under pressure per the farmdoc analysis published Friday. **Class III milk fell hard to $15.67**, down 8.0%, the second-biggest move on today's board and one that dairy producers shouldn't ignore. That is a 52-week low area print and the kind of drop that changes forward contracting math for fall milk. The hog complex running 11% put livestock in the headlines, but the dairy signal is louder for producers actually pricing milk.
sections.catalyst: Cargill lockout ongoing; USDA beef production cut 150M lbs; Class III milk at 52-week low area.
sections.bottom_line: Dairy producers: Class III at $15.67 is a warning, not background noise.

sections.title: Hormuz Back on the Tape
sections.body: WTI crude settled at **$71.41**, down 1.4% on the session, which requires a direct read: oil prices ran 4% in Asian trade Monday morning on Hormuz escalation headlines, then gave most of it back as the session progressed. Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, just reversed. Tanker traffic through the Strait fell to a five-week low on Sunday per OilPrice.com, transponders are going dark again, and Iran struck five Gulf nations hosting U.S. forces over the weekend. The crude market opened with risk and closed with doubt. Natural gas fell to **$2.94**, down 2.3%, even as European TTF opened 3% higher on Hormuz LNG risk. U.S. natural gas is insulated from Hormuz flows in the short run because U.S. LNG exports route differently. **The diesel and crop-input cost story is now live again** if Hormuz stays closed more than a week. Watch the Tuesday crude open for confirmation.
sections.catalyst: U.S.-Iran escalation, Iranian strikes on five Gulf nations, Hormuz tanker traffic at five-week low.
sections.bottom_line: Crude opened higher, closed lower. Hormuz is back as a live risk, not a resolved one.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Settlement Locks In Right-to-Repair for Farmers
outside_the_pit.body: An FTC settlement with John Deere, confirmed this weekend, secures farmers' right to repair their own equipment without voiding warranties or losing dealer access. This is a structural win for input cost management: independent repair shops and on-farm fixes are now protected. The National Farmers Union called it a real commitment, not just another promise.

outside_the_pit.title: "Product of USA" Label Gains Momentum With 10 New Companies
outside_the_pit.body: USDA Secretary Rollins announced 10 new meat and poultry companies have joined the voluntary Product of USA labeling initiative. This is a slow-building trade story: as the China $17 billion annual U.S. ag purchase commitment, announced May 18, works through the ratification uncertainty, domestic labeling that differentiates U.S. product at retail becomes a parallel demand lever.

outside_the_pit.title: 75% of U.S. Cowherd Still in Drought-Affected Areas
outside_the_pit.body: With 75% of the nation's cowherd in active drought areas per the Fence Post, herd rebuild math stays difficult even as bred heifer interest is rising. Drought-stressed pastures are accelerating cull decisions, which puts near-term beef supply pressure against a tighter long-term supply picture. The WASDE already cut 2026 beef output 150 million pounds; this drought persistence is the structural reason why.

spread_to_watch.label: Corn nearby / Dec '26 carry spread
spread_to_watch.commentary: Nearby corn at $4.38 against December at $4.61 is a 23-cent carry that widened on a single session where old crop fell hard and new crop held. That spread is the market's real-time pollination risk gauge: today's crop progress at 3 PM CT either justifies or deflates it. If condition ratings hold above 65% good/excellent, expect this carry to compress toward 15 cents by Friday.

basis.headline: Old-crop corn basis firming as nearby futures pressure eases.
basis.body: With nearby corn futures falling hard today, **cash basis at country elevators is firming in the Eastern Belt** as merchandisers bid against the weakening futures board to attract old-crop bushels before storage charges mount. Western Belt basis is softer, consistent with available storage and no immediate transportation pressure. Soybean basis is steady to slightly firm on crush demand; the meal and oil complex moving higher is keeping processors active at current levels.

weekly_thread.question: Will today's crop progress condition rating justify December corn's $4.61 hold above nearby?
weekly_thread.status_text: The corn nearby/December split opened Monday at 23 cents, the widest it has been, with the entire thesis resting on pollination-weather risk. Today's USDA Crop Progress at 3 PM CT is the first hard data point of the week: condition ratings above 65% good/excellent compress the spread; below 60% confirms the weather premium. Every other grain story this week reads through that lens.

the_more_you_know.title: When the Strait Closes: How Hormuz Moves Your Input Costs
the_more_you_know.body: The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly 20% of global LNG and a significant share of crude, and tanker traffic just fell to a five-week low as transponders went dark again. That's not a grain story directly, but it becomes one inside 30 to 60 days. Diesel, nitrogen fertilizer, and crop-protection chemicals all trace back to natural gas and crude feedstocks. When Hormuz premium rebuilds, the sequence runs: crude higher, gas higher, ammonia and urea higher, then fall input bid sheets tighten. Producers locking fall anhydrous or UAN contracts this week are pricing before that sequence plays out. Today's WTI close at $71.41 is down on the day but opened 4% higher this morning: the market hasn't decided yet whether this escalation sticks.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn condition rating below 60% good/excellent adds weather premium, December toward $4.75; above 65%, nearby pressure wins and the nearby/Dec spread compresses.
WTI crude: if it opens above $73 on sustained Hormuz closure confirmation, diesel and input cost story goes live for fall applications.
Weekly Export Sales: soybeans below 300K MT keeps the chart in charge; above 400K MT confirms China's $17 billion purchase commitment is moving physical bushels, not just headlines.
Lean hogs: watch whether $94.78 holds or gives back more than 5 cents on profit-taking. A 10.8% session that doesn't follow through tells you it was fund rotation, not a demand signal.
Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout: any resolution announcement immediately lifts feeder cattle and compresses the live/feeder spread.

daily_quote.text: Don't put all your eggs in one basket.

source_summary: CME Group closing prices; USDA WASDE July 2026; USDA Crop Progress; OilPrice.com Hormuz reporting; Brownfield Ag News; Feedstuffs; farmdoc daily; Farm Policy News; The Fence Post; EIA crude production data.
